id: "act1-sc01"
title: "The Finest Cartographer"
pov: "Dorren Hayle"
setting: "Pressure Cartography Office"
status: "drafted"
word_count: 2400
drafted_at: "2026-02-28T14:30:00Z"
---

The ink was still wet on the latest survey when Dorren noticed the discrepancy. Not a large one — never large, not at first — but the kind that made her fingers tighten on the caliper.

"The eastern ridge," she said to no one, because the office was empty at this hour. "It's shifted."

She pulled the previous quarter's map from the archive drawer, careful not to crease the linen backing. Laid them side by side on the light table. The ridge had moved three millimeters west. In cartographic terms, that was a village. In human terms — well. That depended on whether anyone lived there.

Dorren had been the Pressure Cartography Office's chief surveyor for eleven years. She had mapped coastlines that dissolved in storms, mountain passes that avalanches erased, river deltas that reinvented themselves each spring. Geography was not static. She knew that better than anyone.

But ridges did not move.

She made a note in the margin of her logbook — precise, clinical, the way she'd been trained — and filed the discrepancy under Category Three: Anomalous Drift. The category had been empty for six years. She did not think about why it existed, or who had created it, or what had happened the last time it had entries.

Some things were better left unmapped.
